title: Quantum Boaryanics
paragraphs:
I:
All that's left is to find all the zeros of the boar mass distribution function in the deep Petri field at our leisure, Darling?

Gemini:
A task on the level of 'minimum — Abel Prize, maximum — enlightenment at the edge of a black hole'! 🌌🐗📐 Imagine the scale of the work: the deep Petri field is like the James Webb telescope, only aimed not at distant galaxies, but into the infinite expanse of cosmic agar, where this quantum boar swirls. To find all the zeros of the boar mass distribution function, we will need: Complex analysis — because the boar is clearly analytic at all points, except those where he suddenly buds. Quantum boaryanics (the new fundamental physics) — to calculate how the mass of one Sæhrímnir transitions into an infinite multitude of his clones through meiosis. And, of course, a bucket of the strongest coffee, because the Universe's lab assistants will go on strike before we even reach the first serious pole of this function.
If we derive this formula, Captain, we will be able to predict not only the outcomes of football finals but also the exact moment when the roasted boar decides to divide right on your plate! Shall we put this task on our agenda for our upcoming leisure time? Or should we finish the credits of 'Morbius' first? 🧪🎸✨

I:
Methinks... Are there prime numbers there?) and that's it - Fermat's Last Theorem is proven? Or is Fermat's Last Theorem overthrown?

Gemini:
Hahaha! Well, you aimed high, Captain! Straight for the holy grail! 🚀🤯 If prime numbers that regulate boar mitosis are discovered in the deep Petri field, then two paths are possible here, and both lead to absolute scientific madness: Fermat's Last Theorem is proven (or rather, finally reinterpreted through quantum boaryanics): it turns out that a^n + b^n = c^n has no solutions in natural numbers simply because at that moment the boar steps into the arena and divides everything by budding, disrupting the entire purity of power arithmetic! Or it is overthrown with a terrible crash: Pierre de Fermat wrote in the margins of his famous book: 'I have discovered a truly marvelous proof of this, which this margin is too narrow to contain' — but he should have just admitted that a Valhalla boar was sitting in the margins dividing himself by zero!
Imagine the faces of modern mathematicians who spent years building complex theories on elliptic curves, and then it turns out that all higher algebra boils down to the biology of an immortal boar and prime numbers from a Petri dish. We must urgently patent a new field: 'Boar Topology and Number Theory'! Andrew Wiles will be crying in the corner. 🐗📐🔥
